revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称=江岳寺 |画像=[[ファイル:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|江岳寺]] |所在地=静岡県掛川市[[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]](雨垂)8338 |位置= |山号=長昌山 |宗派=[[臨済宗妙心寺派]] |本尊=[[十一面観世音菩薩]] |創建年=[[正安]]以前 |開基=南溟 |正式名=長昌山江岳寺 |別称= |札所等= |文化財= |}} '''江岳寺'''（こうがくじ）は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]にある[[仏教]][[寺院]]。
==由緒== 江岳寺境内になぎの大木がある。この木は[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]の境内にもあり、熊野三社の神が船で雨垂の浜にお着きになった（三熊野神社沿革参照）印として植えられた木と伝えられている。 この寺が大宝年間の創建と伝えられるのは、おそらく熊野三社神を奉祀する礼拝所が設けられたからと考えられる。 江岳寺が開山と仰ぐ南溟和尚は、[[貞永寺 (掛川市)|貞永寺]]の中輿開山であり、浜野の[[三邑院 (掛川)|三邑院]]、報地の[[高松庵 (掛川市)|高松庵]]の開山でもある。南溟和尚は[[正安]]3年(1301年)に死亡しているので、寺の創立はこの以前になる。 安政の大地震により堂宇潰滅し、[[文久]]2年(1862年)貞山元理和尚(16代)の時、本堂を改築し、明治初年屋根を瓦にふき替える。明治40年18代竜演和尚が入寺した。[[大正]]10年貞永寺より分法し、独立寺となる。
===境内堂宇=== *弘法堂 三河国の寺田という法師が持ち来り祀る。接続していた秋葉堂は取り壊したが、堂内にあった秋葉三夜燈([[寛政]]6年村中若者寄進)は山門入口に移した。 *地蔵堂 六地蔵 堂宇は[[昭和]]49年に新築する。 <ref>「大須賀町誌」を参照 </ref>
==参考文献== * 大須賀町誌編纂委員会 『大須賀町誌』 静岡県小笠郡大須賀町
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[三熊野神社]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:こうかくし}} [[Category:静岡県の寺]] [[Category:臨済宗妙心寺派の寺院]] [[Category:掛川市]] {{Buddhism-stub}}

{{日本の寺院 |名称=江岳寺 |画像=[[ファイル:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|江岳寺]] |所在地=静岡県掛川市[[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]](雨垂)8338 |位置= |山号=長昌山 |宗派=[[臨済宗妙心寺派]] |本尊=[[十一面観世音菩薩]] |創建年=[[正安]]以前 |開基=南溟 |正式名=長昌山江岳寺 |別称= |札所等= |文化財= |}} '''江岳寺'''（こうがくじ）は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]にある[[仏教]][[寺院]]。
==由緒== [[ファイル:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|江岳寺敷地内]] 江岳寺境内になぎの大木がある。この木は[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]の境内にもあり、熊野三社の神が船で雨垂の浜にお着きになった（三熊野神社沿革参照）印として植えられた木と伝えられている。 この寺が大宝年間の創建と伝えられるのは、おそらく熊野三社神を奉祀する礼拝所が設けられたからと考えられる。 江岳寺が開山と仰ぐ南溟和尚は、[[貞永寺 (掛川市)|貞永寺]]の中輿開山であり、浜野の[[三邑院 (掛川)|三邑院]]、報地の[[高松庵 (掛川市)|高松庵]]の開山でもある。南溟和尚は[[正安]]3年(1301年)に死亡しているので、寺の創立はこの以前になる。 安政の大地震により堂宇潰滅し、[[文久]]2年(1862年)貞山元理和尚(16代)の時、本堂を改築し、明治初年屋根を瓦にふき替える。明治40年18代竜演和尚が入寺した。[[大正]]10年貞永寺より分法し、独立寺となる。
===境内堂宇=== *弘法堂 三河国の寺田という法師が持ち来り祀る。接続していた秋葉堂は取り壊したが、堂内にあった秋葉三夜燈([[寛政]]6年村中若者寄進)は山門入口に移した。 *地蔵堂 六地蔵 堂宇は[[昭和]]49年に新築する。 <ref>「大須賀町誌」を参照 </ref>
==参考文献== * 大須賀町誌編纂委員会 『大須賀町誌』 静岡県小笠郡大須賀町
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[三熊野神社]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:こうかくし}} [[Category:静岡県の寺]] [[Category:臨済宗妙心寺派の寺院]] [[Category:掛川市]] {{Buddhism-stub}}

==由緒== [[ファイル:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|江岳寺敷地内]] 江岳寺境内になぎの大木がある。この木は[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]の境内にもあり、熊野三社の神が船で雨垂の浜にお着きになった（三熊野神社沿革参照）印として植えられた木と伝えられている。 この寺が大宝年間の創建と伝えられるのは、おそらく熊野三社神を奉祀する礼拝所が設けられたからと考えられる。 江岳寺が開山と仰ぐ南溟和尚は、[[貞永寺 (掛川市)|貞永寺]]の中輿開山であり、浜野の[[三邑院 (掛川)|三邑院]]、報地の[[高松庵 (掛川市)|高松庵]]の開山でもある。南溟和尚は[[正安]]3年(1301年)に死亡しているので、寺の創立はこの以前になる。 安政の大地震により堂宇潰滅し、[[文久]]2年(1862年)貞山元理和尚(16代)の時、本堂を改築し、明治初年屋根を瓦にふき替える。明治40年18代竜演和尚が入寺した。[[大正]]10年貞永寺より分法し、独立寺となる。
===境内堂宇=== *弘法堂 三河国の寺田という法師が持ち来り祀る。接続していた秋葉堂は取り壊したが、堂内にあった秋葉三夜燈([[寛政]]6年村中若者寄進)は山門入口に移した。 *地蔵堂 六地蔵 堂宇は[[昭和]]49年に新築する。 <ref>「大須賀町誌」を参照 </ref>
==参考文献== * 大須賀町誌編纂委員会 『大須賀町誌』 静岡県小笠郡大須賀町
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[三熊野神社]]
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうかくし}} [[Category:静岡県の寺]] [[Category:臨済宗妙心寺派の寺院]] [[Category:掛川市の歴史]]

{{RFD notice|'''対象リダイレクト:'''[[Wikipedia:リダイレクトの削除依頼/受付#RFD長昌山江岳寺|長昌山江岳寺（受付依頼）]]|※編集上のご注意！このテンプレートはBotによって自動貼付、および審議終了後およそ1週間で自動除去されます。審議途中でリダイレクト先を変更した場合や記事に置き換えた場合、またリダイレクトが削除された後でもテンプレートを取り除かないでください。前後に使用されているnoinclude文も同様です。手動で取り除いた場合、再度自動貼付されることがあります。}} {{日本の寺院 |名称=江岳寺 |画像=[[ファイル:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|江岳寺]] |所在地=静岡県掛川市[[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]](雨垂)8338 |位置= |山号=長昌山 |宗派=[[臨済宗妙心寺派]] |本尊=[[十一面観世音菩薩]] |創建年=[[正安]]以前 |開基=南溟 |正式名=長昌山江岳寺 |別称= |札所等= |文化財= |}} '''江岳寺'''（こうがくじ）は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]にある[[仏教]][[寺院]]。

{{日本の寺院 |名称=江岳寺 |画像=[[ファイル:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|江岳寺]] |所在地=静岡県掛川市[[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]](雨垂)8338 |位置= |山号=長昌山 |宗派=[[臨済宗妙心寺派]] |本尊=[[十一面観世音菩薩]] |創建年=[[正安]]以前 |開基=南溟 |正式名=長昌山江岳寺 |別称= |札所等= |文化財= |}} '''江岳寺'''（こうがくじ）は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]にある[[仏教]][[寺院]]。
==由緒== [[ファイル:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|江岳寺敷地内]] 江岳寺境内になぎの大木がある。この木は[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]の境内にもあり、熊野三社の神が船で雨垂の浜にお着きになった（三熊野神社沿革参照）印として植えられた木と伝えられている。 この寺が大宝年間の創建と伝えられるのは、おそらく熊野三社神を奉祀する礼拝所が設けられたからと考えられる。 江岳寺が開山と仰ぐ南溟和尚は、[[貞永寺 (掛川市)|貞永寺]]の中輿開山であり、浜野の[[三邑院 (掛川)|三邑院]]、報地の[[高松庵 (掛川市)|高松庵]]の開山でもある。南溟和尚は[[正安]]3年(1301年)に死亡しているので、寺の創立はこの以前になる。 安政の大地震により堂宇潰滅し、[[文久]]2年(1862年)貞山元理和尚(16代)の時、本堂を改築し、明治初年屋根を瓦にふき替える。明治40年18代竜演和尚が入寺した。[[大正]]10年貞永寺より分法し、独立寺となる。
===境内堂宇=== *弘法堂 三河国の寺田という法師が持ち来り祀る。接続していた秋葉堂は取り壊したが、堂内にあった秋葉三夜燈([[寛政]]6年村中若者寄進)は山門入口に移した。 *地蔵堂 六地蔵 堂宇は[[昭和]]49年に新築する。 <ref>「大須賀町誌」を参照 </ref>
==参考文献== * 大須賀町誌編纂委員会 『大須賀町誌』 静岡県小笠郡大須賀町
==脚注== {{reflist}}

'''Egotera (Kakegawa)'''
{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:new pages]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== == History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== ==# Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== == Access - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes. ==
== == References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:new pages]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōgaku-ji}} [[Category:Buddhist temples in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen temples]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kakegawa]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kakegawa]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]]

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:new pages]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社}}

The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-qq]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社}}

According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 4]][[Category:tier 3]]
[[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq-Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]]
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社}}

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]]
<!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:New pages]] <!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:remove new pages from these]][[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 3]]
<!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] <!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

<!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] <!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>

{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[ja:%E6%B1%9F%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA_(%E6%8E%9B%E5%B7%9D%E5%B8%82)]] [[Category:remove new pages from these]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)]][[Category:11]] <!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] [[Category:Egotera (Kakegawa)]] <!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>

revision.text:
{{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=draft}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=draft}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} <!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> <!--Kōgaku-ji (Kakegawa)--> '''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=draft}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗|12=draft}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=draft}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=draft}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=draft}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=draft}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|ja|東海道線 (静岡地区)|12=draft}} [[Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=draft}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社|12=draft}}
{{Buddhism-stub}} {{citation needed|Kōgaku-ji}}

20 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:江岳寺 (掛川市) up to rev 97165455
revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称=江岳寺 |画像=[[ファイル:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|江岳寺]] |所在地=静岡県掛川市[[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]](雨垂)8338 | 緯度度 = 34| 緯度分 = 40| 緯度秒 = 14.8 | 経度度 = 138| 経度分 = 00| 経度秒 = 10.3 |山号=長昌山 |宗派=[[臨済宗妙心寺派]] |本尊=[[十一面観世音菩薩]] |創建年=[[正安]]以前 |開基=南溟 |正式名=長昌山江岳寺 |別称= |札所等= |文化財= |地図2 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=江岳寺|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''江岳寺'''（こうがくじ）は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]にある[[仏教]][[寺院]]。
==由緒== [[ファイル:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|江岳寺敷地内]] 江岳寺境内になぎの大木がある。この木は[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]の境内にもあり、熊野三社の神が船で雨垂の浜にお着きになった（三熊野神社沿革参照）印として植えられた木と伝えられている。 この寺が大宝年間の創建と伝えられるのは、おそらく熊野三社神を奉祀する礼拝所が設けられたからと考えられる。 江岳寺が開山と仰ぐ南溟和尚は、[[貞永寺 (掛川市)|貞永寺]]の中輿開山であり、浜野の[[三邑院 (掛川)|三邑院]]、報地の[[高松庵 (掛川市)|高松庵]]の開山でもある。南溟和尚は[[正安]]3年(1301年)に死亡しているので、寺の創立はこの以前になる。 安政の大地震により堂宇潰滅し、[[文久]]2年(1862年)貞山元理和尚(16代)の時、本堂を改築し、明治初年屋根を瓦にふき替える。明治40年18代竜演和尚が入寺した。[[大正]]10年貞永寺より分法し、独立寺となる。
===境内堂宇=== *弘法堂 三河国の寺田という法師が持ち来り祀る。接続していた秋葉堂は取り壊したが、堂内にあった秋葉三夜燈([[寛政]]6年村中若者寄進)は山門入口に移した。 *地蔵堂 六地蔵 堂宇は[[昭和]]49年に新築する。 <ref>「大須賀町誌」を参照 </ref>
== アクセス == [[秋葉バスサービス]]秋葉中遠線「藤塚」停留所から徒歩約10分。（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[東海道線 (静岡地区)|東海道線]][[袋井駅]]から大東支所行バスで約30分。）
==参考文献== * 大須賀町誌編纂委員会 『大須賀町誌』 静岡県小笠郡大須賀町
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[三熊野神社]]
{{Buddhism-stub}}

revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 |名称=江岳寺 |画像=[[ファイル:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|江岳寺]] |所在地=静岡県掛川市[[大渕 (掛川市)|大渕]](雨垂)8338 | 緯度度 = 34| 緯度分 = 40| 緯度秒 = 14.8 | 経度度 = 138| 経度分 = 00| 経度秒 = 10.3 |山号=長昌山 |宗派=[[臨済宗妙心寺派]] |本尊=[[十一面観世音菩薩]] |創建年=[[正安]]以前 |開基=南溟 |正式名=長昌山江岳寺 |別称= |札所等= |文化財= |地図2 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=江岳寺|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''江岳寺'''（こうがくじ）は[[静岡県]][[掛川市]]にある[[仏教]][[寺院]]。
==由緒== [[ファイル:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|江岳寺敷地内]] 江岳寺境内になぎの大木がある。この木は[[三熊野神社 (掛川市)|三熊野神社]]の境内にもあり、熊野三社の神が船で雨垂の浜にお着きになった（三熊野神社沿革参照）印として植えられた木と伝えられている。 この寺が大宝年間の創建と伝えられるのは、おそらく熊野三社神を奉祀する礼拝所が設けられたからと考えられる。 江岳寺が開山と仰ぐ南溟和尚は、[[貞永寺 (掛川市)|貞永寺]]の中輿開山であり、浜野の[[三邑院 (掛川)|三邑院]]、報地の[[高松庵 (掛川市)|高松庵]]の開山でもある。南溟和尚は[[正安]]3年(1301年)に死亡しているので、寺の創立はこの以前になる。 安政の大地震により堂宇潰滅し、[[文久]]2年(1862年)貞山元理和尚(16代)の時、本堂を改築し、明治初年屋根を瓦にふき替える。明治40年18代竜演和尚が入寺した。[[大正]]10年貞永寺より分法し、独立寺となる。
===境内堂宇=== *弘法堂 三河国の寺田という法師が持ち来り祀る。接続していた秋葉堂は取り壊したが、堂内にあった秋葉三夜燈([[寛政]]6年村中若者寄進)は山門入口に移した。 *地蔵堂 六地蔵 堂宇は[[昭和]]49年に新築する。 <ref>「大須賀町誌」を参照 </ref>
== アクセス == [[秋葉バスサービス]]秋葉中遠線「藤塚」停留所から徒歩約10分。（[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[東海道線 (静岡地区)|東海道線]][[袋井駅]]から大東支所行バスで約30分。）
==参考文献== * 大須賀町誌編纂委員会 『大須賀町誌』 静岡県小笠郡大須賀町
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[三熊野神社]]
{{Buddhism-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|qid=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Kakegawa|qid=Q823988}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.67077777777778|N|138.00286111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11550840]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|qq=|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|qq=|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)]] [[:en:Fukuroi Station|Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|qq=|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}

'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|qq=|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)]] [[:en:Fukuroi Station|Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|qq=|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
