'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|qq=|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>

'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.67077777777778|N|138.00286111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11550840]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)]] [[:en:Fukuroi Station|Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}

'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)]] [[:en:Fukuroi Station|Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}

The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>

'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)]] [[:en:Fukuroi Station|Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.67077777777778|N|138.00286111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11550840]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka]], [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in [[:en:Kakegawa, Shizuoka|Kakegawa City]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai Zen]], and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)]] [[:en:Fukuroi Station|Fukuroi Station]], take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:江岳寺 (掛川市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Myōshin-ji sect|WD=Q108821667}}|prefecture={{ill|Kakegawa|WD=Q823988}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.67077777777778|N|138.00286111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11550840]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:20, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Japanese Buddhist Temple |name = Kōgaku-ji (江岳寺) |image = [[File:江岳寺（掛川市）.JPG|300px|Kōgaku-ji]] |location = {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|en|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|WD=Q823988|lt=Kakegawa City}}, [[Ōbuchi (Kakegawa)|Ōbuchi]] (Amadare) 8338 |latitude = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 14.8 |longitude = 138|longitude_minutes = 00|longitude_seconds = 10.3 |mountain_name = Chōshōzan (長昌山) |sect = {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} |main_image = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}} |founded = Before the Shōan era (1299) |founder = Nanmei (南溟) |official_name = Chōshōzan Kōgaku-ji (長昌山江岳寺) |other_name = |cultural_property = |map = {{location map|Japan Shizuoka Prefecture|label=Kōgaku-ji|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }}
'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|en|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|WD=Q823988|lt=Kakegawa City}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|en|Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|WD=Q11527981|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)}} {{ill|Fukuroi Station|en|Fukuroi Station|WD=Q871711|lt=Fukuroi Station}}, take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}

'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|en|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|WD=Q823988|lt=Kakegawa City}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|en|Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|WD=Q11527981|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)}} {{ill|Fukuroi Station|en|Fukuroi Station|WD=Q871711|lt=Fukuroi Station}}, take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|江岳寺 (掛川市)|version=97165455|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Kōgaku-ji''' (江岳寺) is a Buddhist temple located in {{ill|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|en|Kakegawa, Shizuoka|WD=Q823988|lt=Kakegawa City|1=Kakegawa, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It belongs to the {{ill|Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|ja|臨済宗妙心寺派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoshin-ji school of Rinzai Zen|1=Myōshin-ji sect|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108821667}} sect of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Zen|1=Rinzai school}}, and its principal image is the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観世音菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 十一面観音|1=Ekādaśamukha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404731}}.
== History == [[File:江岳寺の碑文.JPG|thumb|200px|Stele within the grounds of Kōgaku-ji]] A large Japanese podocarpus tree (nagi) stands on the temple grounds. According to legend, a similar tree is found at {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}, marking the spot where the deities of the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|lt=Three Kumano Shrines|ja|熊野三社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} arrived by boat at Amadare Beach (see the history of Mikumano Shrine).
The temple is believed to have been founded during the Taihō era (701–704), as it was established as a place of worship for the Kumano deities. The temple’s founder, Nanmei Oshō (南溟和尚), was also the founder of {{ill|Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja|貞永寺 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōei-ji (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja|三邑院 (掛川)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San'yū-in (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja|高松庵 (掛川市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsu-an (Kakegawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. He passed away in Shōan 3 (1301), so the temple’s origin predates this time.
During the [[:en:1854 Ansei-Tōkai earthquake|Ansei earthquake]] (1854), the temple was destroyed. It was rebuilt in 1862 (Bunkyū 2) by the 16th abbot, Teizan Genri (貞山元理). In the early Meiji era, the roof was tiled. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Ryūen Oshō (竜演和尚), the 18th abbot, took over the temple. In 1921 (Taishō 10), the temple became independent from Jōei-ji.
=== Buildings on the Temple Grounds === * '''Kōbō-dō (弘法堂)''' – The statue of Kōbō Daishi was brought here by a priest from Terada in Mikawa Province. The adjacent Akiba-dō was dismantled, but its Akiba San’ya Lantern (dedicated by local youths in Kansei 6, 1794) was moved to the temple gate. * '''Jizō-dō (地蔵堂)''' – A hall housing the Six Jizō statues. The current building was constructed in 1974 (Shōwa 49). <ref>See "Ōsuka Town History" (大須賀町誌)</ref>
== Access == - From [[:en:JR Central|JR]] {{ill|Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|en|Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)|WD=Q11527981|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR Central)|1=Tōkaidō Line (Shizuoka area)}} {{ill|Fukuroi Station|en|Fukuroi Station|WD=Q871711|lt=Fukuroi Station|1=Fukuroi Station}}, take the {{ill|Akihabara Bus Service|ja|秋葉バスサービス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akihabara Bus Service|1=Akiha Bus Service|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595813}} (Akiba Chūen Line) bound for Daitō Branch Office (大東支所) and get off at "Fujitsuka" (藤塚) bus stop (approx. 30 minutes), then walk for about 10 minutes.
== References == * Ōsuka Town History Compilation Committee. *Ōsuka Town History* (大須賀町誌). Ōsuka Town, Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|ja|三熊野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumano Shrine (Kakegawa)|qq=}}
{{Buddhism-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|江岳寺 (掛川市)|version=97165455|comment=Imported full ja history}}
